remarriage.Hinduism: Marriage is a sacred samskara. The Bhagavad Gita frames separation and union in spiritual terms — the soul's bond with the Divine is the model for marital covenant.
remarriage.Christianity: The Decalogue's adultery prohibition and Jesus's teaching in Matthew 19 establish marriage as an unbreakable covenant before God. Paul offers pastoral guidance for widowhood.
remarriage.Islam: The Quran permits remarriage after divorce (talaq) with waiting periods (iddah). Righteousness includes family obligation — remarriage must preserve duties to children and kin.
remarriage.Judaism: The Torah's adultery prohibition requires a formal get (writ of divorce) before remarriage. The covenant model of the Shema governs all sacred bonds.
remarriage.Sikhism: Guru Granth Sahib teaches that God dwells within all. Spiritual peace through Naam supersedes external marital status — remarriage is accepted with compassion.
remarriage.Buddhism: The Dhammapada teaches that hatred is appeased by love alone. Attachment, not marriage, causes suffering. Remarriage is a personal choice guided by wisdom.

divorce.Hinduism: The Mahabharata presents Draupadi's story as a complex case of marital obligation. The BG teaches equanimity in all circumstances.
divorce.Christianity: Jesus teaches that what God has joined, let no one separate. The Beatitudes comfort those who mourn — including mourning broken covenants.
divorce.Islam: Talaq is permitted but disliked. The Quran emphasizes reconciliation and specifies waiting periods to ensure careful deliberation.
divorce.Judaism: Divorce is permitted through the get. The Torah's laws govern the process as a legal and moral obligation.
divorce.Sikhism: Guru Granth Sahib acknowledges separations occur. The focus is on dignity, fairness, and spiritual well-being of both parties.
divorce.Buddhism: Non-attachment is the path. Compassion and honest communication guide the dissolution of relationships.

marriage.Hinduism: Marriage is a sacred samskara — an eternal bond. The BG teaches devotion and duty as the foundation of all relationships.
marriage.Christianity: Marriage is a covenant reflecting Christ's relationship with the Church. 'Honor your father and mother' establishes the family unit as sacred.
marriage.Islam: Nikah is a sacred contract. The Quran calls spouses 'garments for one another.' Righteousness includes care for family.
marriage.Judaism: Marriage (kiddushin) is a holy covenant. The Shema establishes monotheistic covenant as the model for all bonds.
marriage.Sikhism: The Anand Karaj ceremony sanctifies marriage. The Guru unites husband and wife in devotion to God.
marriage.Buddhism: Marriage is a social institution. The emphasis is on mutual respect, fidelity, and shared spiritual practice.

death.Hinduism: The soul transmigrates. The BG teaches: 'As a person puts on new garments, giving up old ones, the soul similarly accepts new material bodies.'
death.Christianity: Death is conquered through resurrection. 'We rejoice in our sufferings, knowing that suffering produces endurance.'
death.Islam: Death is a transition to akhirah. 'Every soul shall taste death, then to Us you will be returned.'
death.Judaism: Focus is on this life's moral obligations. The covenant continues beyond death.
death.Sikhism: The soul passes through cycles of birth and death until it merges with God through Naam meditation.
death.Buddhism: Death is part of samsara. 'You yourselves must make the effort; the Buddhas only show the way.'

forgiveness.Hinduism: Forgiveness is a divine virtue. The BG describes the devotee who is 'forgiving' as dear to God.
forgiveness.Christianity: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.' Forgiveness is both received and extended.
forgiveness.Islam: Al-Ghafur (The Forgiving) is one of Allah's names. 'If you pardon and forgive, Allah is Forgiving, Merciful.'
forgiveness.Judaism: Teshuvah (repentance) leads to forgiveness. Yom Kippur is the Day of Atonement.
forgiveness.Sikhism: Forgiveness comes through Naam and Guru's grace. 'Forgive the mistakes of all beings.'
forgiveness.Buddhism: 'Hatred is never appeased by hatred. By love alone is hatred appeased.'

suffering.Hinduism: Suffering arises from ignorance and attachment. The BG teaches equanimity in pleasure and pain.
suffering.Christianity: Suffering has redemptive power. 'Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted.'
suffering.Islam: Suffering is a test. 'Do people think they will be left alone because they say, We believe?'
suffering.Judaism: Covenant faithfulness persists even in trials. The Shema is declared in all circumstances.
suffering.Sikhism: Suffering comes from ego and separation from God. 'Through pain, the Word is understood.'
suffering.Buddhism: Dukkha is the First Noble Truth. It arises from craving. The Eightfold Path is the way out.

love.Hinduism: Prema (divine love) is the highest emotion. The BG teaches devotion to God as the path to liberation.
love.Christianity: 'God is love. Whoever lives in love lives in God.' Agape — unconditional love — is the greatest virtue.
love.Islam: 'He loves them and they love Him.' Love of Allah and His creation is central to faith.
love.Judaism: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself.' Ahavah is the foundation of covenant.
love.Sikhism: 'Through love, the disease of egotism is removed. Through love, all sorrows are dispelled.'
love.Buddhism: 'Hatred is never appeased by hatred. By love alone is hatred appeased.'

peace.Hinduism: Shanti is inner peace from realizing the Divine. The BG teaches equanimity as the path to stillness.
peace.Christianity: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you.' Divine peace transcends understanding.
peace.Islam: Salam is one of Allah's names. Peace comes through submission to the Divine will.
peace.Judaism: Shalom is wholeness. The vision is universal peace: 'Nation shall not lift up sword against nation.'
peace.Sikhism: 'Meditating on the Lord of the Universe, I find peace. By the Guru's grace, all sorrows depart.'
peace.Buddhism: 'Mind precedes all mental states. If with a pure mind a person acts, happiness follows.'

prayer.Hinduism: Puja and japa connect the soul to the Divine. Meditation and devotion are forms of prayer.
prayer.Christianity: 'Pray without ceasing.' Prayer is communion with God — the model is the Lord's Prayer.
prayer.Islam: Salat (five daily prayers) connects the servant directly to Allah without intermediary.
prayer.Judaism: Tefillah connects the individual to God. The Shema declares oneness; the Amidah is the central prayer.
prayer.Sikhism: Naam Simran is the core practice. 'Remember the Lord, and all thy fears shall depart.'
prayer.Buddhism: Meditation and chanting cultivate mindfulness. Buddhist prayer is reflection and recitation.
